blocks:
Ayahs

أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ (١)
Did We not open your breast for you (1)
وَوَضَعْنَا عَنكَ وِزْرَكَ (٢)
and relieve you of your burden (2)
ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنقَضَ ظَهْرَكَ (٣)
which [almost] broke your back? (3)
وَرَفَعْنَا لَكَ ذِكْرَكَ (٤)
Did We not exalt your name? (4)
فَإِنَّ مَعَ ٱلْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا (٥)
Indeed ease accompanies hardship. (5)
إِنَّ مَعَ ٱلْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا (٦)
Indeed ease accompanies hardship. (6)
فَإِذَا فَرَغْتَ فَٱنصَبْ (٧)
So when you are done, appoint, (7)
وَإِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَٱرْغَب (٨)
and supplicate your Lord. (8)

(Commentary)

This is a command to exert oneself for the sake of Allah and to turn toward Him with longing, preceded by a mention of His favors. The Surah could potentially be either Meccan or Medinan, though the context of its verses is more consistent with the Medinan period.

In some narrations from the Imams of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام), it is stated that al-Duha and Alam Nashrah constitute a single Surah. This is also narrated from Tawus and Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz. Al-Razi stated in al-Tafsir al-Kabir, after citing them: "What led them to this view is that His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ (Did We not open your breast for you?) [94:1] appears as a conjunction to His saying: أَلَمْ يَجِدْكَ يَتِيماً (Did He not find you an orphan?) [93:6]. However, this is not the case, because the former was revealed during a state of grief for the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) due to the persecution of the disbelievers—a state of trial and constriction of the breast—whereas the latter necessitates that the state at the time of revelation be one of an expanded breast and a tranquil heart. How then can the two be joined?" (End of quote).

The critique of this is that the "expansion of his breast" (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) mentioned in the verse refers to making it such that it can encompass the realities (الحقائق) cast into it, and not be constricted by the divine gnosis (المعارف) revealed to him or the harm he suffers from people while conveying them, as will be explained. It does not merely mean tranquility of heart and joy as he interpreted it.

This is supported by what Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Sa'id ibn Jubayr, from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I asked my Lord a question that I later wished I had not asked. I said: "O Lord, there were prophets before me, among whom was one for whom You subjected the wind, and among whom was one who gave life to the dead." He said: "Did I not find you an orphan and give you shelter?" I said: "Yes." He said: "Did I not find you wandering and guide you?" I said: "Yes, O Lord." He said: "Did I not expand for you your breast and remove from you your burden?" I said: "Yes, O Lord."'" There is a completion to this discussion that will reach you in the commentary on Surah al-Ilaf, if Allah (the Exalted) wills.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ (Did We not open your breast for you?) [94:1]. Al-Raghib said: "The linguistic root of al-sharh (الشرح) is the spreading out of meat and the like. It is said: 'I sliced the meat' (شرحت اللحم). From this comes 'the expansion of the breast' (شرح الصدر), meaning its expansion by a divine light (نور إلهي), a tranquility (سكينة) from Allah, and a spirit (روح) from Him. The Exalted said: رَبِّ اشْرَحْ لِي صَدْرِي (My Lord! Open my breast for me) [20:25], أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ (Did We not open your breast for you?) [94:1], and فَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ (Is he whose breast Allah has opened...) [39:22]." (End of quote).

The thematic sequence of the first three verses, followed by their justification in His saying: فَإِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْراً (For indeed with hardship goes ease) [94:5]—which clearly applies to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) circumstances during the beginning, middle, and end of his mission—then the repetition of the justification, and finally the derivation of the two verses at the end of the Surah; all of this testifies that the intended meaning of "expanding his breast" (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is its enlargement so that it may encompass the revelation (الوحي) cast into it and which he is commanded to convey, as well as the hardships and harm he suffers for the sake of Allah. In other words, it means making his sacred self (النفس المقدسة) fully prepared to receive what is emanated upon it from the side of Allah (the Exalted).

His saying, the Exalted: وَوَضَعْنا عَنْكَ وِزْرَكَ الَّذِي أَنْقَضَ ظَهْرَكَ (And take off from you your burden, which had weighed heavily on your back?) [94:2-3]. The burden (الوزر) is a heavy load. The "weighing heavily on the back" (إنقاض الظهر) refers to it being strained such that a sound is heard from it, similar to the sound heard from a bed or the like when a heavy object is placed upon it. What is meant is the manifestation of the weight of the burden upon him to an extreme degree.

"Removing the burden" (وضع الوزر) means taking away the sensation of its weight. The sentence وَوَضَعْنا عَنْكَ وِزْرَكَ (And take off from you your burden) is conjoined to His saying: أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ (Did We not open...), because the meaning is: "We have indeed expanded your breast for you."

The meaning of removing his burden (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), according to what the context suggests—as we have indicated—is the facilitation of his mission and the success of his struggle for the sake of Allah through the alignment of causes. For the message (الرسالة), the call (الدعوة), and their consequences are the "weight" he carried following the expansion of his breast.

It has been said that "removing the burden" refers to what is mentioned in narrations: that two angels descended upon him, split his chest, took out his heart, purified it, and then returned it to its place. This narration will reach you shortly.

It has also been said that the "burden" refers to what occurred from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) before his mission. Others said it refers to his unawareness of the laws (الشرائع) and similar matters that depend on revelation while he was seeking them. It was also said to be his perplexity regarding certain matters like fulfilling the rights of the messengership. Some said it refers to the revelation (الوحي) and its weight upon him at the beginning of his affair. Others said it was what he saw of his people's misguidance and stubbornness while he was unable to guide them. It was also said to be what he saw of their transgression and excessive harm toward him. Some said it was his grief over the death of his uncle Abu Talib and his wife Khadijah. It was also said that the "burden" refers to sin, and its removal refers to his infallibility (عصمته). Finally, it was said that the "burden" refers to the sins of his community, and its removal refers to their forgiveness.

Some of these views are absurd and others are weak, failing to align with the context; they range from mere assertions to mere possibilities.

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَفَعْنا لَكَ ذِكْرَكَ (And exalted for you your name?) [94:4]. Exalting the mention (رفع الذكر) means raising it above the level of the mention of other people. The Glorified has indeed done this for him. Among the ways His mention was exalted is that Allah coupled his name (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with His own Name; thus, his name is joined with the Name of his Lord in the two testimonies (الشهادتين) which are the foundation of Allah's religion. Every Muslim must mention him along with his Lord every day in the five obligatory prayers. It is a subtle grace that this "exaltation" (الرفع) occurs after the "removal" (الوضع) in the two verses.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْراً (For indeed with hardship goes ease) [94:5]. It is not improbable that this is a justification for what preceded regarding the removal of the burden and the exaltation of his mention. The messengership he carried and the call he was commanded with—which is the heaviest burden a human can possibly carry—had become intense for him. Likewise, his people's denial of his call, their belittling of him, and their persistence in trying to erase his mention had become intense. Thus, Allah removed the burden he carried by granting success to the people in responding to his call, and He exalted his mention which they sought to erase. This was in accordance with His (the Exalted) sunnah (السنة) — the established divine way — in the cosmos of bringing ease after hardship. Thus, He justified the removal of hardship from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by referring to this sunnah. Accordingly, the definite article (ال) in "the hardship" (العسر) denotes the genus (الجنس) rather than total exhaustion (الاستغراق). Perhaps the sunnah is the law of the transformation of events and the changing of states, and their lack of permanence.

It is narrated from al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf that the "fa" (ف) in فَإِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ (For indeed with hardship...) is an elided conditional (فصيحة), and the speech is intended to console him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with a beautiful promise.

He said: "The polytheists used to taunt the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers for their poverty and distress, until it occurred to his noble mind that they turned away from Islam because of the poverty and insignificance of its adherents. So the Glorified reminded him of the magnificent favors He had bestowed upon him, then said: إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْراً (Indeed, with hardship goes ease). It is as if He said: 'We have granted you what We have granted you, so do not despair of Allah's grace, for indeed with the hardship you are in, there is ease.'"

The apparent meaning of this is that the definite article in "hardship" (العسر) refers to a specific known instance (للعهد) rather than the genus, and that the "ease" refers to the abundant spoils Allah granted the believers later.

This is rejected, for his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) noble mind is too exalted for their condition to be hidden from him, or for him not to know that they only turned away from his call out of arrogance against the truth and haughtiness toward Allah. Furthermore, the people did not desire Islam even after its power became manifest and the believers became wealthy. Allah had already made His Prophet despair of the faith of most of them when He said: لَقَدْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلى أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (The word has certainly become due against most of them, so they will not believe... [to His saying]) وَسَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not believe) [36:7-10], and these verses are Meccan. He also said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ (As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not believe) [2:6], and this verse is Medinan.

However, if the "ease after hardship" is interpreted as the power and elevation of Islam after its weakness, while considering the Surah to be Meccan, there would be no great harm in that.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْراً (Indeed with hardship goes ease) [94:6]. This is a repetition for emphasis (التأكيد) and consolidation (التثبيت). It has also been said to be a new sentence (استئناف). They mentioned that in the two verses there is an indication that with one hardship there are two eases, based on the principle that if a definite noun (المعرفة) is repeated in speech, the second refers to the same as the first, unlike an indefinite noun (النكرة). For example, if it is said: "If you earn the dirham (الدرهم) or a dirham (درهما), then spend the dirham (الدرهم)," the second refers to the first. This is unlike saying: "If you earn a dirham, then spend a dirham." However, this rule is not universally applicable.

The nunation (التنوين) in يُسْراً (ease) is for categorization (للتنويع), not for magnification (للتفخيم) as some have mentioned. The "with-ness" (المعية) is a with-ness of succession (توالي), not a with-ness in the sense of occurring at the same point in time.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا فَرَغْتَ فَانْصَبْ وَإِلى رَبِّكَ فَارْغَبْ (So when you are done, appoint, and turn eagerly to your Lord) [94:7-8]. This is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) derived from what was explained before regarding the burden of the messengership and the call, and Allah's favor upon him through the expansion of the breast, the removal of the burden, and the exaltation of his mention—all of which are instances of ease after hardship.

Accordingly, the meaning is: since ease follows hardship and the matter rests with Allah alone, then when you have finished what was made obligatory upon you, exhaust yourself for the sake of Allah—through His worship and supplication—and turn toward Him with longing so that He may favor you with the rest that follows this exhaustion and the ease that follows this hardship.

It has been said: the meaning is "when you finish the obligatory prayers, exert yourself in the supererogatory ones." It was also said: "when you finish the prayer, exert yourself in supplication." What these two views contain are merely some of the instances of application (المصاديق).

It was also said: the meaning is "when you finish the battle, strive in worship." It was said: "when you finish with your worldly affairs, exert yourself for your hereafter." Other views have been mentioned, but they are weak.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd Allah ibn Ahmad narrated in Zawa'id al-Zuhd from Ubayy ibn Ka'b that Abu Hurayrah said: "O Messenger of Allah, what was the first thing you saw of the matter of prophethood?" The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sat up straight and said: "You have asked, O Abu Hurayrah. I was in the desert at the age of twenty years and some months, when suddenly I heard speech above my head, and there was a man saying to another: 'Is it he?' They approached me with faces the like of which I had never seen on any creature, and spirits the like of which I had never found in any creature, and garments the like of which I had never seen on anyone. They came walking toward me until each of them took hold of my upper arm, yet I did not feel the touch of either of them.

One of them said to his companion: 'Lay him down.' So he laid me down without force or pressure. One of them said: 'Split his breast.' One of them reached for my breast and split it, as far as I could see, without blood or pain. He said to him: 'Remove the rancor and envy.' He took out something like a blood clot and threw it away. Then he said to him: 'Insert compassion and mercy.' Then something like what he had removed, resembling silver, was inserted. Then he moved the thumb of my right foot and said: 'Go forth and be safe.' I returned, going forth with tenderness for the small and mercy for the great."

I say: In the report of some—as in Ruh al-Ma'ani—it says "ten years" instead of "twenty years and some months." In some narrations, the story is reported at the time of the revelation of Surah Iqra' bi-ismi Rabbika, and in others—as in Sahih al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Nasa'i—the story is reported at the time of the Prophet's Night Journey (الإسراء).

In any case, the story is undoubtedly of the nature of imaginal representation (التمثل). Scholars have engaged in lengthy discussions to justify its contents as a physical event, resorting to contrived explanations that are useless to address once their fundamental premise is shown to be flawed.

In the same source, Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in al-Dala'il narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Gabriel came to me and said: 'Indeed your Lord says: Do you know how I exalted your mention?' I said: 'Allah knows best.' He said: 'Whenever I am mentioned, you are mentioned with Me.'"

In the same source, Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Jarir, al-Hakim, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from al-Hasan who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came out one day happy and laughing, saying: 'One hardship shall never overcome two eases: فَإِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْراً إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْراً (For indeed with hardship goes ease. Indeed with hardship goes ease).'"

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا فَرَغْتَ فَانْصَبْ وَإِلى رَبِّكَ فَارْغَبْ (So when you are done, appoint, and turn eagerly to your Lord), it means: "When you finish the obligatory prayer, exert yourself toward your Lord in supplication and turn to Him with longing in your request." He said: "This is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abd Allah (عليه‌السلام)."
